nodes.label: Rippled Web App
nodes.description: Main SPA — React + Vite frontend served by FastAPI
nodes.pseudologic:
• React Router initializes with auth-guarded routes
• useAuth() checks Supabase session on mount
• Authenticated users see Main/Shortlist/Commitments tabs
• Admin users get additional /admin routes
• API calls go through apiClient with auth headers
• Vite dev server proxies /api to FastAPI backend
nodes.broader_context: The single entry point for all user interaction with Rippled. Users manage commitments, review signals, and configure integrations entirely through this app.

nodes.label: Create User
nodes.description: Supabase Auth sign-up flow
nodes.pseudologic:
• User enters email + password on sign-up form
• Calls Supabase Auth signUp() method
• Supabase creates auth user and sends confirmation email
• On confirmation, user row created in public schema
• Redirects to guided onboarding flow
nodes.broader_context: Creates the user account that all commitment tracking is anchored to. Without sign-up, nothing else in the system activates.

nodes.label: Login / Forgot Password
nodes.description: Supabase Auth login + password recovery
nodes.pseudologic:
• User enters email + password
• Calls Supabase Auth signInWithPassword()
• On success, stores session token and redirects to dashboard
• Forgot password triggers resetPasswordForEmail()
• Password reset link sent via Supabase email template
nodes.broader_context: Gate that ensures only authenticated users access their commitment data. Handles the session lifecycle that all API calls depend on.

nodes.label: User Details
nodes.description: Account settings — display name, email preferences
nodes.pseudologic:
• Loads current user profile from /api/v1/users/me
• Displays editable fields: display name, notification preferences
• Saves changes via PATCH to user settings endpoint
• Updates local auth context on success
nodes.broader_context: Lets users control how they appear in the system and how they receive notifications about their commitments.

nodes.label: Integrations
nodes.description: Connect Email, DMs, Calendar, Meetings. Manage LLM token.
nodes.pseudologic:
• Lists available connectors: Email (IMAP), Slack, Read.ai, Calendar
• Each connector shows connect/disconnect status
• OAuth flows for Slack; credential entry for IMAP/API keys
• LLM API key (OpenAI) stored encrypted in user_settings
• Credentials saved via /api/v1/integrations endpoint
nodes.broader_context: The critical setup step — without integrations connected, no source items flow in and no commitments are ever detected. This is where the data pipeline begins.

nodes.label: Seed Data Approval
nodes.description: Per-channel approval before initial seed pass processes historical data
nodes.pseudologic:
• User sees list of available channels/sources after connecting
• Each channel requires explicit approval before historical scan
• Approved channels trigger seed detection task via Celery
• Seed detector processes historical messages through the same detection pipeline
• Results appear as commitments with older timestamps
nodes.broader_context: Prevents the system from overwhelming users with hundreds of historical commitments on first connect. Users control which channels get backfilled.

nodes.label: Guided Onboarding
nodes.description: Step-by-step wizard for new users — integrations, identity, seed
nodes.pseudologic:
• Multi-step wizard with progress indicator
• Step 1: Connect at least one integration (email/Slack)
• Step 2: Confirm identity — verify name/email for owner resolution
• Step 3: Approve seed channels for historical backfill
• Marks onboarding complete in user settings on finish
• Redirects to main dashboard (Active screen)
nodes.broader_context: Ensures new users set up the minimum viable configuration so the system can start detecting commitments immediately. Without this, users would land on an empty dashboard.

nodes.label: Profile Resolution
nodes.description: Identity profile system — maps emails/names to confirmed user identities
nodes.pseudologic:
• User confirms their known names/emails during onboarding
• Stored as UserIdentityProfile rows (name, email, source)
• Owner resolver matches extracted owner strings against profiles
• Uses exact match, substring match, then fuzzy match (threshold 0.75)
• Resolves suggested_owner to a confirmed user_id or leaves unresolved
nodes.broader_context: Makes it possible to attribute commitments to the right person. Without identity resolution, the system can't determine who owns a detected commitment.

nodes.label: Active State
nodes.description: Main dashboard — Active tab showing highest-priority commitments
nodes.pseudologic:
• Fetches commitments from /api/v1/commitments with surfaced_as filter
• Groups by context (thread/source) with inline badges
• Sorted by priority_score descending
• Each card shows: deliverable, owner, counterparty, deadline, confidence
• User actions: confirm, dismiss, snooze (not now), skip
• Actions update lifecycle_state via PATCH endpoint
nodes.broader_context: The primary screen users see daily. This is where commitment intelligence becomes actionable — users review what the system found and decide what matters.

nodes.label: Source Item Ingested
nodes.description: Raw message/event stored as source_item from any connector
nodes.pseudologic:
• Webhook/poller delivers raw message to /api/v1/source-items
• Validates source ownership (user must own the source)
• Creates SourceItem row with content, sender, timestamps, metadata
• Deduplicates by external_id to prevent double-processing
• Fires detect_commitments Celery task asynchronously
• Returns created source item ID
nodes.broader_context: The funnel entry point for all raw communication data. Every email, Slack message, and meeting transcript enters the system here before any intelligence is applied.

nodes.label: Candidate Detection
nodes.description: Determines if a source item contains a commitment-like signal using hybrid detection (rule-based + LLM)
nodes.pseudologic:
• Receives source item content from Celery task
• Two-pass detection: rule-based pattern matching first, then LLM
• Rule-based: regex patterns for 'I will', 'follow up', etc. per source type
• Suppression patterns filter out greetings, sign-offs, pleasantries
• LLM call (GPT-4.1-mini): system prompt defines commitment taxonomy
• LLM performs self-check pass — validates its own extractions against reject-first rules
• Returns ModelDetectionResult with is_commitment, confidence, explanation
• Logs every run to detection_audit with prompt_version for eval tracking
nodes.broader_context: The core intelligence of Rippled. This is where raw text becomes structured commitment candidates. Detection quality directly determines whether users trust the product.
open_questions: Should delegated asks be first-class before acceptance?

nodes.label: Signal Classification
nodes.description: Classify signal_type (self_commitment, delegated_action, follow_up_intent, etc.)
nodes.pseudologic:
• Takes detected commitment candidate as input
• Classifies into signal types: self_commitment, delegated_action, follow_up_intent
• May run as separate LLM call or be folded into detection prompt
• Output informs surfacing priority (delegated actions score differently)
• Classification stored on commitment row for filtering
nodes.broader_context: Adds nuance beyond 'is this a commitment?' — understanding the type of commitment changes how it should be prioritized and presented to the user.
open_questions: Separate classifier or part of detection prompt?

nodes.label: Structured Extraction
nodes.description: Extracts owner, deliverable, deadline, counterparty from detected signals
nodes.pseudologic:
• Part of the same LLM call as detection (single prompt, structured JSON output)
• Extracts: suggested_owner, deliverable text, deadline (if mentioned), counterparty
• LLM returns structured JSON with all fields
• Deadline parsing normalizes relative dates ('next week', 'Friday') to absolute
• Missing fields left as null — system tolerates partial extraction
• All extracted fields are 'suggested values' — user confirms later
nodes.broader_context: Turns a vague 'yes this is a commitment' signal into actionable structured data. The extracted fields (who, what, when, for whom) are what users actually see and act on.

nodes.label: Owner Resolution
nodes.description: Maps extracted owner name/email to confirmed identity profiles
nodes.pseudologic:
• Takes suggested_owner string from structured extraction
• Loads all UserIdentityProfile rows for the user
• Tries exact match on name/email fields
• Falls back to substring match (either direction)
• Falls back to fuzzy match using SequenceMatcher (threshold 0.75)
• Returns resolved user_id or None if no match found
nodes.broader_context: Bridges the gap between what an LLM extracts ('John will handle this') and who that person actually is in the system. Critical for showing commitments to the right user.

nodes.label: Surfacing Decision
nodes.description: Decides whether commitment goes to Main, Shortlist, or held for triage based on priority scoring
nodes.pseudologic:
• Runs classifier to get dimension scores (externality, timing, consequence, burden, confidence)
• Computes priority_score 0-100 via weighted formula
• Checks observation window — holds commitment if still in silent period
• Routes by score: ≥60 → Main, ≥35 → Shortlist, <35 → held internally
• If critical ambiguity detected and score ≥25 → Clarifications surface
• Returns RoutingResult with surface destination and human-readable reason
nodes.broader_context: The quality gate between detection and user visibility. Prevents low-confidence noise from reaching users while ensuring high-stakes commitments surface immediately.

nodes.label: Commitment Created / Held for Triage
nodes.description: Final pipeline output — commitment row created in DB, surfaced or held
nodes.pseudologic:
• Surfacing sweep runs periodically via Celery task
• Loads all commitments in active lifecycle states (proposed, active, needs_clarification)
• Pre-computes proximity map: hours until delivery events per commitment
• For each commitment: classify → score → route
• Updates surfaced_as, priority_score, dimension scores on commitment row
• Appends SurfacingAudit row if surface destination changed
• Returns summary dict with counts of changes made
nodes.broader_context: The final step where a detected signal becomes visible (or stays held). This is the boundary between the AI pipeline and the user-facing product.

nodes.label: Proposed
nodes.description: Initial state — system detected a commitment candidate
nodes.pseudologic:
• Default lifecycle_state when a commitment row is first created
• Commitment exists in DB but may not yet be visible to user
• Eligible for surfacing evaluation on next sweep
• Can transition to: surfaced (meets threshold), dismissed, or remain proposed
nodes.broader_context: The starting point for every commitment in the system. A 'proposed' commitment is the system's hypothesis that hasn't yet been validated by scoring or the user.

nodes.label: Surfaced (Active / Commitments)
nodes.description: Commitment visible on Main or Shortlist tabs
nodes.pseudologic:
• Commitment passed surfacing threshold (priority_score ≥ 35)
• surfaced_as set to 'main' or 'shortlist'
• Now visible in user's dashboard with full card details
• User can take action: confirm, dismiss, snooze, skip
nodes.broader_context: The moment the system's work becomes visible to the user. This is the product's core value delivery — surfacing what matters before it falls through the cracks.

nodes.label: Confirmed
nodes.description: User explicitly confirmed this is a real commitment
nodes.pseudologic:
• User clicks 'Confirm' on a surfaced commitment card
• PATCH /api/v1/commitments/{id} with lifecycle_state='confirmed'
• Commitment moves from suggested to user-validated
• Remains visible and tracked until delivered or closed
• Confirmation signal fed back to improve detection quality
nodes.broader_context: User validation that the AI got it right. Confirmed commitments are the highest-trust items in the system and anchor the user's workflow.

nodes.label: Dormant (Not Now)
nodes.description: User snoozed — will resurface later
nodes.pseudologic:
• User clicks 'Not Now' on a surfaced commitment
• PATCH endpoint sets lifecycle_state='dormant'
• Commitment hidden from active views
• May resurface on next surfacing sweep if conditions change
• Allows users to defer without permanently dismissing
nodes.broader_context: Gives users control over timing without losing track. A snoozed commitment isn't forgotten — it comes back when it becomes relevant again.

nodes.label: Skipped
nodes.description: User chose to skip without dismissing — may revisit
nodes.pseudologic:
• User clicks 'Skip' on a surfaced commitment
• PATCH endpoint sets lifecycle_state='skipped'
• Removed from active views but not permanently dismissed
• Weaker signal than 'Not Now' — user unsure rather than deferring
• Available in history for later review
nodes.broader_context: A soft pass that doesn't count against detection quality. Users who aren't sure about a commitment can skip it without consequence.

nodes.label: Dismissed
nodes.description: User marked as not-a-commitment — discarded from view
nodes.pseudologic:
• User clicks 'Dismiss' on a surfaced commitment
• PATCH endpoint sets lifecycle_state='dismissed'
• Permanently removed from active views
• Strong negative signal — this was a false positive
• Dismissal data used by LLM Judge to evaluate detection quality
nodes.broader_context: The user's way of telling the system it got it wrong. Dismissed commitments are critical training signal for improving detection accuracy over time.

nodes.label: Delivered
nodes.description: System detected completion evidence — auto-matched to open commitment
nodes.pseudologic:
• Monitor incoming source items for completion signals
• Match completion evidence to open confirmed commitments
• Matching strategies: thread-based > semantic similarity > manual
• Auto-transition lifecycle_state to 'delivered' on match
• User can confirm or reject the delivery detection
nodes.broader_context: Closes the loop on commitment tracking. Without delivery detection, users must manually mark every commitment as done, which defeats the purpose of automated tracking.
open_questions: Delivery detection matching strategy: thread > semantic > manual

nodes.label: Closed
nodes.description: User confirmed obligation is fully resolved
nodes.pseudologic:
• User confirms that a delivered commitment is truly complete
• Terminal lifecycle state — no further transitions
• Commitment archived but retained for analytics
• Contributes to reliability scoring and trust metrics
nodes.broader_context: The final state confirming the obligation cycle is complete. Closed commitments are the measure of the system's end-to-end value.

nodes.label: Detection Audit
nodes.description: Logs every detection run — source item, parsed result, prompt version, model used
nodes.pseudologic:
• Every model_detection.py call writes a DetectionAudit row
• Stores: source_item_id, raw LLM response, parsed_result JSON, model name
• Records prompt_version used for that run
• Enables before/after comparison when prompts change
• Queryable by admin review UI for spot-checking
nodes.broader_context: The accountability layer for AI decisions. Without audit logs, there's no way to understand why the system made a particular detection decision or track quality over time.

nodes.label: LLM Judge
nodes.description: Weekly quality review — Anthropic Claude evaluates detection accuracy, triggers prompt improvement WOs
nodes.pseudologic:
• Runs weekly via Celery scheduled task
• Pulls recent DetectionAudit rows as evaluation sample
• For each audit row: sends source content + model output to Claude Sonnet
• Judge prompt asks: missed commitments? false positives? quality rating 1-5?
• Parses structured JSON response from judge
• Accumulates results into LlmJudgeRun aggregate row
• If average quality < threshold, flags for prompt improvement
• Incorporates user feedback (SignalFeedback) for ground truth comparison
nodes.broader_context: The automated quality assurance system. Uses a stronger model (Claude Sonnet) to evaluate the detection model's (GPT-4.1-mini) output, creating a feedback loop that drives prompt improvements.

nodes.label: Eval Harness
nodes.description: Regression testing for detection quality — runs against golden set
nodes.pseudologic:
• Maintains a golden set of labeled source items with expected outputs
• Runs detection pipeline against each golden item
• Compares actual vs expected: precision, recall, F1
• Reports regressions when a prompt change degrades quality
• Can be triggered manually or as part of prompt change CI
nodes.broader_context: Prevents prompt improvements from accidentally breaking cases that already work. The safety net that gives confidence to iterate on detection quality.

nodes.label: Prompt Registry
nodes.description: Version-controlled prompt files under ops/prompts/ with governance rules
nodes.pseudologic:
• Prompt files stored as markdown in ops/prompts/ directory
• Each prompt versioned with naming convention (e.g., detection-prompt-v6.md)
• PROMPT_GOVERNANCE.md defines change process and review requirements
• _PROMPT_VERSION constant in code references the active version
• Changes require eval harness run + LLM judge review before deploy
nodes.broader_context: Treats prompts as first-class code artifacts with version control and governance. Prevents ad-hoc prompt changes from degrading detection quality in production.

nodes.label: Email (IMAP Poller)
nodes.description: Polls IMAP inbox for new messages, creates source_items
nodes.pseudologic:
• Celery periodic task polls IMAP server for new messages
• Authenticates using encrypted credentials from user_settings
• Fetches unread messages since last poll timestamp
• Parses email: extracts body, sender, recipients, thread_id
• Creates SourceItem row per email with source_type='email'
• Marks messages as seen to avoid re-processing
nodes.broader_context: One of the primary data sources for commitment detection. Email is where many business commitments are made, especially with external stakeholders.

nodes.label: Slack (Connector)
nodes.description: Slack webhook + OAuth connector for DM/channel messages
nodes.pseudologic:
• OAuth2 flow connects user's Slack workspace
• Receives real-time events via Slack Events API webhook
• Processes message events from approved channels/DMs
• Extracts: message text, sender, channel, thread_ts, timestamp
• Creates SourceItem row per message with source_type='slack'
• Handles Slack URL verification challenge on webhook setup
nodes.broader_context: Captures the fast-moving informal commitments made in chat. Slack messages are often where 'I'll handle that' happens but gets forgotten without tracking.

nodes.label: Read.ai (Webhook)
nodes.description: Meeting transcript webhook from Read.ai — creates source_items from transcript
nodes.pseudologic:
• Receives webhook POST from Read.ai after meeting ends
• Payload contains full meeting transcript with speaker labels
• Segments transcript into logical conversation blocks
• Creates SourceItem rows with source_type='meeting'
• Includes meeting metadata: title, attendees, duration
• Triggers detection pipeline on each segment
nodes.broader_context: Captures commitments made in live meetings — the highest-value and most commonly forgotten source. Meeting action items are where Rippled provides the most differentiated value.

nodes.label: Calendar
nodes.description: Google/Outlook calendar integration for deadline correlation
nodes.pseudologic:
• Will connect to Google Calendar / Outlook Calendar APIs
• Polls for upcoming events and deadlines
• Creates Event rows for delivery deadline correlation
• Enables proximity spike scoring (C3) for approaching deadlines
• Links calendar events to commitments via CommitmentEventLink
nodes.broader_context: Adds temporal intelligence — knowing when a commitment's deadline is approaching changes its priority. Calendar integration turns static commitments into time-aware obligations.
